content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES CorvilvussloN
P.O. Box 807
. 428 J Street
. Sacramento. CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660
. Fax (916) 322-0886
February 23, 1999
Jonathan Smith
San Francisco Bay Conservation and Development Commission
Thirty Van Ness Avenue, Suite 2011
San Francisco, California 94102-6080
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-98-321
Dear Mr. Smith:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1 You are requesting advice on behalf ofHarry Yahata, an
alternate member of the San Francisco Bay Conservation and Development Commission.
(“BCDC”).
QUESTION
May Mr. Yahata, a District Director for the California Department of Transportation
(“Caltrans”), participate in any way, including voting, on a Caltrans application for the San
Mateo Bridge project or any other Caltrans project that comes before the BCDC?
CONCLUSION
Mr. Yahata may make and participate in decisions that involve his public employer,
Caltrans, because he is not being compensated by Caltrans for his participation.
FACTS
The BCDC consists of 27 members, each of whom is appointed and each of whom can
appoint an alternate to participate in all BCDC activities, including voting on permit
applications. The California Business, Housing, and Transportation Agency appoints one of
Government Code sections 81000 -91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109
-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-98-321
Page 2
BCDC’s Commissioners. Currently, the Business, Housing, and Transportation Agency
appointed Commissioner is John Bama, the Deputy Secretary. His alternate is Mr. Yahata.
The BCDC is a state land use agency charged with the regulation ofthe placement of fill,
the extraction ofmaterials, and any substantial change in use within the BCDC’s jurisdiction,
which includes San Francisco bay and its immediate shoreline. One of the BCDC’s major
functions is to review and act on applications for permits to authorize such activities. Currently
the BCDC is considering Application No. 20-98, which was submitted by Caltrans, seeking
authorization for the seismic retrofitting and addition of a second parallel bridge structure
immediately north of the eastern portion of the bridge structure.
Mr. Yahata is a District Director for Caltrans. The BCDC meetings are held during
regular work hours. Mr. Yahata is paid a per diem from the BCDC to compensate him for the
time he spends serving the BCDC. He turns this per diem over to Caltrans.
You have asked whether Mr. Yahata may participate in any way regarding this Caltrans
application, or any other Caltrans project that comes before the BDCD.
ANALYSIS
Your letter raises two potential Political Reform Act issues. First, whether Mr. Yahata is
prohibited from making or participating in Caltrans decisions under the conflict of interest
provisions of the Act. (Sections 87100-87 103.) Second, whether Mr. Yahata is prohibited by the
prohibition on public officials imposed by section 87104.
Conflicts of Interest Pursuant to Sections 87100 - 87103
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. In addition, the Act requires every public official to
disclose all his or her economic interests that could foreseeably be affected by the exercise of the
official’s duties. (Sections 81002(c), 87200-87313.)
Members of the BCDC qualify as public officials pursuant to Section 82048 and
Regulation 18701. Therefore, the disclosure and disqualification requirements of the Act apply
to BCDC members.


File No. A-98-321
Page 3
Section 87103 provides that a public official has a financial interest in a decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on the official or a member of his or her immediate family
or on:
“(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and other than loans by a
commercial lending institution in the regular course ofbusiness on terms available
to the public without regard to official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, received by or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.” (Section
87103(c).)
While sources of income are potentially disqualifying economic interests, salary and
reimbursement for expenses or per diem received from a state, local, or federal government
agency are not regarded as income for purposes of the Act. (Section 82030(b)(2).) Therefore,
Mr. Yahata’s salary from Caltrans will not constitute a disqualifying source of income within the
meaning of Section 87103(c). Mr. Yahata may participate in decisions affecting Caltrans unless
another law prohibits it.
Section 87104
Section 87104, which became effective January 1, 1995, and amended in 1997, provides:
“(a) No public official of a state agency shall, for compensation, act as an agent
or attorney for, or otherwise represent, any other person by making any formal or
informal appearance before, or by making any oral or written communication to,
his or her state agency or any officer or employee thereof, ifthe appearance or
communication is made for the purpose of influencing action on a contract, grant,
loan, license, permit, or other entitlement for use.”
“Person” is broadly defined in Section 82047 to include any individual, proprietorship,
firm, partnership, joint venture, syndicate, business trust, company, corporation, association,
committee, and any other organization or group ofpersons acting in concert. This definition has
been construed to include public agencies within its scope. (Geismar Advice Letter No.
A-95-407, citing In re Witt (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 1 and Evans Advice Letter, No. 1-86-117.)
Accordingly, we have advised that the prohibition in Section 87104 applies to compensation
made by public agencies. (Travis Advice Letter, No. A-96-021.)
Therefore, Mr. Yahata may not for compensation act as an agent or attorney for, or
otherwise represent Caltrans, by making any formal or informal appearance before, or by malcing
any oral or written communication to, the BCDC or any officer or employee of the BCDC, if the
appearance or communication is made to influence the agency’s action on a. contract, grant, loan,
license, permit, or other entitlement for use.


File No. A-98-321
Page 4
Your facts indicate that the BCDC meetings are held during regular work hours. Mr.
Yahata is paid a per diem from the BCDC to compensate him for the time he spends serving on
the BCDC. He turns this per diem over to Caltrans. As such, it is clear that Mr. Yahata is being
paid by BCDC for his time and effort serving on the BCDC. He is not being paid to act as an
agent or attorney for, or otherwise represent, Caltrans or any other person. Therefore, Mr.
Yahata may make and participate in decisions that relate to Caltrans while serving on the BCDC.
Incompatible Activities
Lastly, this letter only addresses Mr. Yahata’s duties under the Political Reform Act. It
does not address other state laws outside our jurisdiction which may preclude Mr. Yahata from
making or participating in issues related to Caltrans. (E.g., Section 1090 et seq., “Incompatible
Activities of State Officers and Employees.”)
Ifyou have any further questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
916/322-5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
SGC:DA:tls
By:
Allison
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May Mr. Yahata, a District Director for the California Department of Transportation
(“Caltrans”), participate in any way, including voting, on a Caltrans application for the San
Mateo Bridge project or any other Caltrans project that comes before the BCDC?

CONCLUSION: Mr. Yahata may make and participate in decisions that involve his public employer,
Caltrans, because he is not being compensated by Caltrans for his participation.
